parse.why: Ionic conductivity of 4.8 mS/cm at 30°C for HCGPE extracted from EIS tests in Results and Discussion section. No applicable selectivity (light olefins not relevant), breakthrough time to t50 (no conversion rate data), or regeneration data found for HCGPE in text. Coulombic efficiency and cycling stability involve composite HCGPE-PE separator and lack direct field mapping in schema.

HCE is explicitly described as an electrolyte for lithium metal batteries (ion exchange scenario) conducting Li+ ions. No schema-matching performance metrics (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration) are reported for HCE itself: ionic conductivity (4.8 mS/cm) and transference number (0.48) are material properties not covered by schema fields; cycling stability data (740h in Li/Li symmetric cell) doesn't match breakthrough definition (t50 for conversion rate drop); no regeneration process is mentioned for HCE. Performance comparisons in full cells (e.g., capacity retention) refer to HCGPE-PE composites, not pure HCE.
Fig. 2d and text in Results and Discussion: "The LAWTP sample has a room temperature ionic conductivity of 1.07 mS/cm" for pure LAWTP solid electrolyte.

Material PVDF-HFP-SN is a solid polymer electrolyte for lithium-ion batteries, with scenario 'ion_exchange' inferred from context as it conducts Li+ ions. Ionic conductivity (1.07e-5 S/cm at 30°C) extracted from Results section (Fig. 4b and text) as the primary capacity-like property; unit compacted to 'S_cm'. No data for selectivity, breakthrough, or regeneration as no catalytic/adsorption properties, breakthrough curves, or regeneration processes described for this material.

No performance data (e.g., capacity, selectivity, breakthrough, regeneration) reported for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions; all quantitative results (ionic conductivity, battery capacity, etc.) focus on PVDF-HFP-SN and PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP. Experimental section only describes synthesis, not application performance.

Capacity value 142.69 mAh/g extracted from Fig. 9b and text ('discharge specific capacities of 142.69 and 136.41 mAh/g in the 1st and 500th cycles') for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP at 0.5C and room temperature. Scenario 'ion_exchange' and target species 'lithium_ions' derived from electrolyte application in lithium-ion batteries context (Results and discussion). Selectivity, breakthrough, and regeneration not applicable or mentioned for this material.

No direct performance data (e.g., ionic conductivity, transference number, cycling capacity) is provided for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions. Only PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP is characterized with specific values (e.g., ionic conductivity at 50°C: 1×10⁻⁴ S/cm, Li⁺ transference number: 0.478, discharge capacity: 142.69 mAh/g). For PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP, the paper only mentions general trends (e.g., reduced ionic conductivity due to aggregation) without numerical extraction.

Results and Discussion section 4.5 explicitly reports an electrochemical window of approximately 8 V for neat [HMG][FSI] at 75°C, measured by cyclic voltammetry with Pt electrodes, indicating its application as a solid electrolyte material.

Paper identifies application as solid polymeric electrolytes for lithium-ion batteries (ion exchange) in Conclusion section, but no quantified performance metrics (e.g., capacity, selectivity, breakthrough, regeneration) are reported for (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09 in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions; data provided are thermal properties (Tg, Td) and qualitative NMR mobility insights.

No application performance metrics (e.g., capacity, selectivity, breakthrough time, regeneration) reported for (PMMA)0.91(TFSI)0.09 in the provided text. Available data in Table 1 (e.g., Tg=64°C, degradation temperatures) are thermal properties from characterization, not classified as application performance under the schema. Sections 3.2 and 3.3 focus on IM14-based systems or general behaviors without specific performance values for this material. Diffusion data in Table 2 and Fig. 7 are exclusively for IM14-gel-like systems, not applicable here.

Paper positions PIL-PMMA systems as candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in lithium-ion batteries (Conclusions section: "PILs as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs"), but no quantitative application performance metrics (e.g., capacity, selectivity, breakthrough time, regeneration data) are reported for (PMMA)0.91(TFO)0.09; only thermal properties (T_g = 57°C, degradation temperatures) are characterized in Section 3.1 and Table 1, which are material properties not mapped to schema fields.

The paper discusses (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 as an IM14-gel-like system for ion dynamics and potential electrolyte applications in energy storage, but reports no specific catalytic, adsorption, separation, or regeneration performance metrics (e.g., capacity, selectivity, breakthrough time, or regeneration temperature/yield) as defined in the schema. Performance data provided (e.g., diffusion coefficients in Table 2) are kinetic parameters not matching schema requirements, and no target species, scenario values (catalysis/adsorption_separation/ion_exchange), or numerical evidence for application properties are explicitly associated with this material in Results, tables, or figure captions.

论文 Conclusions 部分明确指出该材料用于锂离子电池 (LIBs) 的固体聚合物电解质（'protic ionic liquids (PILs) as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs'），涉及 Li+ 离子传导，故 scenario 为 'ion_exchange'，target_species 为 'Li+'。Table 2 和 Fig. 7 报告了自扩散系数等动力学数据，但未提供 schema 定义的应用性能数值（如 capacity, selectivity, breakthrough, regeneration），且无相关实验条件或再生信息。

The provided text focuses on the synthesis and characterization of monomer M1 (Pentafluorophenylacrylate) and its polymer PM1, but no direct application performance data (e.g., catalysis, adsorption, ion exchange) for M1 itself is reported in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions. Performance metrics in Table 1, Table 2, and associated figures pertain exclusively to polymer analogous reactions of PM1 (the polymer derived from M1), not to M1 as a standalone material. Experimental section details synthesis conditions but lacks application-oriented performance values for M1.

The paper reports application-like performance data (e.g., conversion rates in Table 1) only for polymers (PM1, PM2, etc.), not for the monomer M2 specified in MATERIAL_ID. M2 (Pentafluorophenylmethacrylate) is described solely in synthesis and characterization contexts (e.g., Experimental section 2.2 and Results section 3.1), with no numerical performance metrics in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions directly tied to M2 as a material for scenarios like catalysis or adsorption.

No application performance data (e.g., catalysis, adsorption/separation, ion exchange) found for PM1. Text focuses on synthesis, characterization, and reactivity as a precursor polymer in polymer analogous reactions (e.g., conversion rates with amines/alcohols), but lacks metrics like capacity (g/g), selectivity (%), breakthrough time (h_t50), or regeneration under defined application scenarios. Performance values in tables (e.g., Table 1, Table 2) describe reaction kinetics/conversions of PM1 itself, not its end-use application performance.

From Table 1 in Results & Discussion section (Section 3.3.1), PM2 showed 65% conversion in polymer analogous reaction with hexylamine after 24 hours at 50°C in DMF; scenario inferred from Scheme 1 and conclusions describing PM2 as a precursor for multifunctional material synthesis.

No capacity, selectivity, breakthrough, or regeneration data is directly associated with pure PFP; regeneration is not mentioned.

Scenario and target species from context of Na-HPILSE in sodium-ion batteries (section 3.3). Ionic conductivity (1.15e-3 S/cm at 25°C) from Results & Discussion (Fig. 3a). Na+ transference number (0.22) from Results & Discussion (Fig. 3d). No breakthrough time or regeneration data reported for Na-HPILSE.

Specific capacity value 154.8 mAh/g extracted from section 3.5 (Results and discussion) for G2 electrolyte in Li||LFP battery at 0.1C after first activation cycle; other fields null due to no relevant data matching schema definitions (e.g., no light olefin selectivity, breakthrough time, or regeneration process).

Section 3.3 reports initial Coulombic efficiency of 61.7% for F1 electrolyte in Li||Cu half-battery at 0.5 mA/cm² and 1 mAh/cm²; no other application performance numerical data (e.g., capacity retention, breakthrough time, regeneration) for F1 is provided in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions.

Paper context identifies F4 as electrolyte for lithium metal batteries (sections 3.3-3.4, keywords). No quantitative data for F4 in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions matching schema fields: capacity (e.g., mAh/g in full cell not reported for F4), selectivity (not applicable), breakthrough (t50 not defined or reported), regeneration (not discussed). Performance discussed qualitatively (e.g., short cycle life <50 h in Li||Li at 0.5 mA/cm²), but no extractable numerical values per schema rules.

Capacity value 155.7 mAh_g from Section 3.5: 'F7 electrolyte delivers higher initial discharge specific capacity 155.7 mAh·g^{-1}' for Li||LFP battery. Selectivity value 95.9%_CE from Section 3.3: 'F7 electrolyte shows an excellent long-term cycling performance with 130 cycles (>500 h) and the average CE is 95.9%' for Li||Cu half-cell at 0.5 mA·cm^{-2} and 1.0 mAh·cm^{-2}. No breakthrough (t50) data provided for F7. No regeneration process (temperature or recovery ratio) described for F7 in the text.

Scenario 'ion_exchange' and target species 'Li+' from keywords 'Cross-linked polymer electrolyte' and electrochemical characterization (Section 2.4, 3.2). Capacity (ionic conductivity) value 5.02e-5 S cm^{-1} at room temperature for FPCSPE 3-40 (based on crosslinked-P(VEC-co-TFEMA) with LiTFSI) from Results section 3.2. No selectivity data (irrelevant to electrolyte). Breakthrough not defined for battery cycling context; capacity retention 70% after 300 cycles but not t50 and not in hours. Regeneration not mentioned in paper.

The provided text only formulates DSLE2 as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte (LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 10:2) but reports no specific application performance data for it. All detailed electrochemical results (e.g., ionic conductivity, stability, battery cycling) focus on DSLE4 and AFPE10; DSLE2 is not tested in any device or assigned numerical metrics in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions. The collective range '4.57-4.74 V' for DSLEs' electrochemical windows lacks individual values for DSLE2, and no 'all/each sample' statements apply uniquely to it.

Scenario extracted from section 2.3 title 'High-voltage lithium-metal batteries' and testing of Li|DSLE4|LCO cells. Target species 'lithium' from section 2.2 Li|Cu and Li|Li cell tests measuring lithium deposition/stripping stability. No capacity, selectivity, breakthrough (defined as time for conversion rate to drop to 50%), or regeneration data directly reported for DSLE4 matching schema properties; e.g., symmetric cell short-circuit at 938 h is failure time, not t50; CE values and capacity retention are cell-level metrics not mapped to schema attributes.

DSLE6 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte (LiTFSI:LiDFOB 6:6 molar ratio) but no specific performance metrics (e.g., electrochemical window value, ionic conductivity, capacity, or regeneration data) are reported for it individually. General trends for the DSLEs group (e.g., electrochemical window 4.57-4.74 V, decreasing ionic conductivity with higher LiDFOB content) are mentioned, but aggregate data cannot be precisely assigned to DSLE6 without explicit values or figure/table readings. Battery testing and detailed characterization focus on DSLE4 and AFPE10.

The paper defines DSLE8 as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio of 4:8 but reports no specific application performance data for DSLE8 in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions; all detailed performance testing and data (e.g., electrochemical window, ionic conductivity, cycling stability) focus exclusively on DSLE4 and AFPE10.

DSLE10 is only mentioned in the formulation section (LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio 2:10) with no specific performance data (e.g., ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, cycling stability) reported for it in the provided text. Performance results and tests focus on DSLE4 and AFPE10.

Section 2.1 reports ionic conductivity (2.12 mS cm^{-1}) and electrochemical stability (4.76 V) for AFPE5, but no schema fields for these metrics. Battery performance data (e.g., capacity retention, cycling stability) not reported for AFPE5 as AFPE10 was selected for further tests per text: 'AFPE10, which balanced ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability, was used for further investigation.' Scenario and target species derived from paper context (keywords: 'High-voltage lithium-metal battery'; electrolyte function for Li+ conduction).

AFPE10 is identified as an electrolyte for lithium-metal batteries (scenario: battery) with lithium ions as the charge carrier (target_species: lithium_ions). Ionic conductivity of 1.88 mS cm^{-1} is directly reported for AFPE10 in Results and Discussion section (Fig. 1e-f). Other properties like selectivity, breakthrough, and regeneration are not applicable or not directly extractable per schema constraints.

AFPE20 was characterized for electrochemical stability (4.95 V) and ionic conductivity (1.31 mS cm^{-1}) in Section 2.1, but not tested in battery applications; Section 2.1 states 'AFPE10, which balanced ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability, was used for further investigation' for high-voltage lithium-metal batteries, and Sections 2.2-2.3 report application data only for AFPE10.

Ionic conductivity value extracted from Results & Discussion section 2.2: 'leading to a highly improved ionic conductivity of 2.36 mS cm^{-1}' for c-GPE-50. Unit normalized to compact form 'mS_cm^{-1}'. No information for selectivity, breakthrough, or regeneration in provided text; scenario and target_species inferred from electrolyte function in ion transport.

F-HV is used as a fluorinated polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction in lithium metal batteries (Sections 2.2, 2.4, 2.5, 2.6). Scenario 'ion_exchange' inferred from Li+ transport function; target species 'Li+' from ionic conductivity and transfer number measurements. No data matches schema definitions for capacity (conversion/adsorption ability), selectivity (light olefins), breakthrough (time for conversion rate drop to 50%), or regeneration (temperature/recovery ratio) as no such properties are reported for F-HV in the provided text.

PL is explicitly described as a solid electrolyte for Li+ conduction in lithium batteries (Sections 2.2-2.3). Ionic conductivity (0.5 mS/cm at 25°C) and electrochemical stability (4.78 V) are reported in Results & Discussion, but no data aligns with schema fields for capacity (e.g., mass-based conversion/adsorption), selectivity, breakthrough time, or regeneration. Performance metrics like discharge capacity are for full cells (LFP||Li), not directly attributable to PL material properties.

论文在Section 2.1明确指出：'the PLL slurry without NH4HCO3 addition (PLL-0) suffers from serious gelation and fails to derive a flat and smooth film (Figure 1c). Therefore, further investigations about PLL-0 will not be conducted in the following analysis.' 后续Results & Discussion（如离子电导率、电化学窗口、电池性能等）仅针对PL、PLL-15和PLL-25，无PLL-0的任何应用性能数据。
Section 2.3 (Battery Performance) and Figure 3e report stable specific discharge capacity of 135.8 mAh/g at 1C for LFP||Li full cell using PLL-15 electrolyte after initial cycles.

Ionic conductivity at 25°C is 0.6 mS cm^{-1} for PLL-25 from Figure 2c and text in Section 2.2: 'The ionic conductivities at 25°C for PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.5, 1.2, and 0.6 mS cm^{-1}, respectively.' Scenario 'ion_exchange' and target species 'Li+' derived from context of ion conduction in solid electrolytes for batteries. Other properties not reported or applicable for PLL-25 in application context.

Paper explicitly states E-FPAESI is used as core material for optical waveguide devices (e.g., 'good candidate as a core material for the optical waveguide devices'). No catalysis, adsorption, separation, or ion exchange applications or performance metrics (e.g., conversion capacity, selectivity, breakthrough time, regeneration) are reported for E-FPAESI in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions.

The provided text describes synthesis and material properties (e.g., Tg, thermal degradation) for FPAESI but reports no application performance data (e.g., catalysis, adsorption, or optical waveguide results) directly for FPAESI; all application results (e.g., optical loss < 0.40 dB/cm) pertain to the ethynyl-terminated derivative E-FPAESI.

Material HBPS-(PTFEMA-b-PPEGMA)_{27} is applied as solid polymer electrolyte in lithium-ion batteries (Section 3.5, 3.9). Lithium-ion transference number (0.26) extracted from Table 2 at 60°C and mapped to selectivity as it represents ion selectivity. No data for capacity (conversion/adsorption ability per schema), breakthrough (adsorption separation context), or regeneration (not mentioned in paper).

LTN of 0.46 for FPE-6 explicitly reported in Results and Discussion section (3.2) as 'the calculated lithium ion transference number (LTN) of FPE-6 is 0.46'; scenario 'ion_exchange' and target species 'lithium_ions' inferred from context of ion transport in lithium batteries (e.g., 'facilitates the ion transport' in Highlights, 'Li ion transport' in text). No capacity, breakthrough, or regeneration data directly tied to FPE-6 found in Results & Discussion, tables, or figure captions; Experimental section used only for synthesis conditions, not performance extraction.
Ionic conductivity of 2.96×10^{-4} S/cm for FPE-6 (material ID PE-6) reported in the conclusions section as a key performance metric of the electrolyte in lithium ion transport.
